source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

The dog bit the man. The man bit back. Neither recovered quickly. Seven stitches for one. Four days of antibiotics for the other. The neighbor filmed it. Nobody pressed charges. The film got 40,000 views. Some comments called it karma. Some called it cruel. The dog was named Gerald. This is not a metaphor. Do not read anything into the name Gerald.

Marcus paid the bill. He left no tip. The waiter watched him go. Outside, Marcus stood by his car for four minutes. He went back inside. He left twelve dollars on the table. The waiter had already gone home. The busboy took the money. He bought a lottery ticket. He did not win. Marcus never found out any of this happened. Neither did the waiter. The twelve dollars completed a loop no one witnessed.

The envelope arrived on a Wednesday. Inside: one key, one index card, no explanation. The index card said only: "Not yet." Elena put the key in a drawer. Thursday passed. Friday passed. On Saturday she opened the drawer. The key was gone. She had not touched it. No one else had a key to her apartment. She checked the drawer again. Empty. She still has the index card. It still says "Not yet." She does not know what this means. Neither do I.

The interview lasted nine minutes. Priya answered every question correctly. The interviewer thanked her. She was not offered the job. No explanation came. She emailed twice. No response. She called once. Voicemail. Six weeks later, the position was listed again. Same title. Same requirements. She applied again. Same nine-minute interview. Same correct answers. Same thank-you. No offer. She has applied four times now. Each interview: nine minutes. She has started timing them. She does not know why it is always nine minutes. The interviewer does not seem to know either.

The cartographer made a map with one deliberate error. She did this every time. Every map she made contained one wrong road, one false river, one invented hill. She called this her signature. No one had ever found the errors. She had made forty-one maps. Forty-one errors, undetected. She kept a separate notebook listing each mistake. The notebook had forty-one entries. On the last page, she wrote: the error in this notebook is that there are only forty entries. She counted again. There were forty-one. She did not know what to do with this. She still does not.

Nora counted her steps every day. Not obsessively. She simply counted. Tuesday: 4,412 steps. Wednesday: 4,412 steps. Thursday: 4,412 steps. She had not planned this. She had not tried to repeat the number. On Friday she walked a different route. She visited her sister. She got lost twice. She walked 4,412 steps. She told no one. On Saturday she stayed inside all day. She paced. She read. She cooked. At midnight she checked: 4,412 steps. She has not told anyone. She does not want it to stop.

The lighthouse had been dark for eleven years. Marcus knew this because he had counted. Every morning he looked out his window: dark. Every evening: dark. On the night of his birthday — his forty-fourth — it turned on. He saw it. He called the coast guard. They said the lighthouse had been decommissioned. He said, yes, but it is on right now. They sent someone. By the time the person arrived, the light was off. The officer said: we see nothing. Marcus said: I have a photograph. He showed them the photograph. In it, the lighthouse was dark. He had taken it while the light was on. He still does not understand the photograph.

Elena found a receipt in her coat pocket. The total was $14.73. The date was March 3rd. She had not been to that store on March 3rd. She checked her bank records. No transaction. She checked again. No transaction on March 3rd. She showed the receipt to her husband. He said he had not been to that store either. She threw the receipt away. The next morning, it was in her coat pocket again. Same total: $14.73. Same date: March 3rd. She threw it away four more times. It came back four more times. On the fifth morning, she kept it. She has kept it for two years. The receipt has not faded. $14.73. March 3rd. She does not shop at that store.

The archivist catalogued 7,000 photographs. Each one showed the same empty chair. Different rooms, different lighting, different decades — but always the same chair. He spent three years on this. He did not find the chair. He searched auction records, estate sales, furniture databases. Nothing matched. In year four, he moved to a smaller apartment. He needed to sell his chair. He posted a photograph online. Within an hour, someone replied: this is the chair from the archive. He checked. It was not. He checked again. It was. He has lived in his new apartment for six months. He does not sit in the chair. He has not returned to the archive. He still owns the chair.

The cartographer drew the same map seven times. Not copies. The same map. He began each one without looking at the last. Every river ended in the same place. Every mountain had the same name. He had never visited the region. He did not know the region's name. On the seventh map, a road appeared. He had not drawn the road. He checked the previous six. Six roads. He had not drawn those either. He mailed the seventh map to a geographic society. They wrote back: this region does not exist. He wrote back: I know. They did not respond. The road on the map leads to a place he has circled. He does not know why he circled it. His handwriting is in the circle. It says: here.

The translator worked on the same sentence for nine days. The sentence had seven words. She translated it into English. She translated it back. The words were different. She tried again. The words were different again. She asked a colleague. He read the original. He said: this sentence has six words. She counted. Seven. He counted. Six. They counted together. Neither could agree. She photographed the page. In the photograph, the sentence had five words. She still has the photograph. She still has the original. They do not have the same number of words. She has not finished the translation. The sentence, in every version, means the same thing. It means: this cannot be translated.

The witness gave three statements. In the first, she said the light was red. In the second, she said the light was green. In the third, she said there was no light. The investigator asked which was true. She said: all three. He asked her to explain. She said: I have explained. He reviewed the statements again. Each one was signed. Each one was dated the same day. The handwriting was the same in all three. The ink was the same. The paper was the same. He could find no inconsistency except the facts. He closed the case. He opened it again six months later. The statements had not changed. He asked to speak with the witness. She had not existed, according to every record he could find. He still has the three statements. They are still signed.

March 3. She does not shop at that store.' The minor differences (e.g., 'She had not been' vs 'She had not been to that store on March 3') are consistent with slight paraphrase variation from the same system.

Both closely mirror the source text's rhythm without adding extra context, use terse sentence structures with minimal elaboration, and employ similar punctuation patterns (semicolons, short declarative sentences). They both render 'he checked—it wasn't, he checked again—it was' in nearly identical ways, and both end with the same staccato series of short statements about his current situation. Indexes 3 and 4 both add the '7,000 photographs' context but differ enough in prose style to suggest different paraphrasers.

Both use 'not merely/as copies,' both include 'He began/started each one anew without glancing/looking at the previous,' both have the exact same sequence for the geographic society exchange ('They replied that this region does not exist. He answered/wrote back: I know. They did not respond.'), and both end with 'His handwriting is inside the circle, and it says: here.' This level of structural and lexical similarity strongly indicates the same paraphraser produced both outputs.

Indexes 3 and 4 share distinctive fabricated additions not present in other versions: both introduce the detail that 'the photograph showed five words' (vs. the original's ambiguity), both add extra narrative steps ('She tried again'), and both conclude with the explicit meaning 'it cannot be translated'/'it is untranslatable.' They also share a similar flowing, elaborated prose style with paragraph-level expansions, distinguishing them from the more faithful Index 2 and the minimalist Index 0.

Outputs 0 and 1 both preserve the original text's minimalist, repetitive structure with nearly identical content in the same order. Output 0 uses short declarative sentences separated by periods, while Output 1 chains the same clauses with semicolons. Both end with the note about the statements being signed, and neither adds significant new material. This punctuation-only stylistic variation is consistent with outputs from the same paraphraser. On the final page of this notebook, she jotted down a paradoxical note: "the error in this notebook is that there are only forty entries." Upon recounting, she found there were, in fact, forty-one entries. This realization left her perplexed, a state she continues to experience.

Nora counted her steps every day, not calculatingly, but simply: Tuesday: 4,412 steps; Wednesday: 4,412 steps; Thursday: 4,412 steps; she hadn’t planned it, hadn’t tried to repeat the number. On Friday she walked in a different direction, visited her sister, got lost twice, walked 4,412 steps, told no one; on Saturday she stayed at home all day, read, cooked, paced about; at midnight she counted: 4,412 steps. She didn’t tell anyone, didn’t want it to stop.

The lighthouse had been dark for eleven years, and Marcus knew this because he had counted. Each morning he looked out his window and saw darkness, and each evening it remained dark. On the night of his forty-fourth birthday, the light suddenly came on. He saw it and called the coast guard. They said the lighthouse had been decommissioned. He replied that it was on right then. They sent someone, but by the time the guest arrived, the light was off. The officer said they saw nothing. Marcus insisted he had a photograph. He showed them the photo. In it, the lighthouse appeared dark, even though he had taken it while the light was on. He still does not understand the photograph.

Elena found a receipt in her coat pocket. It showed a total of $14.73 and the date March 3. She had not been to that store on March 3. She checked her bank records—there were no transactions—and a second check confirmed there were none on March 3 either. She showed the receipt to her husband, who said he had not been to that store either. She threw the receipt away. The next morning, it was back in her coat pocket, still showing the same total, $14.73, and the same date, March 3. She discarded it again, four more times, and it returned four more times. On the fifth morning, she kept it. She has held onto it for two years. The receipt has not faded. $14.73. March 3. She does not shop at that store.

An archivist compiled 7,000 photographs, each depicting the same empty chair. Various rooms, lighting, and eras—yet the chair remained constant. He spent three years on the project and never found the chair itself. He searched auction records, estate sales, and furniture databases; nothing matched. In the fourth year, he moved to a smaller apartment and needed to sell the chair. He posted a photo online. Within an hour, someone replied: this is the chair from the archive. He checked; at first it didn’t match. He checked again; it did. He has lived in his new apartment for six months. He does not sit in the chair. He has not returned to the archive. He still owns the chair.

Seven times the cartographer drew the same map—not as copies, but the exact same map. He started each one anew without looking at the previous one. Every river ended at the same point, every mountain bore the same name. He had never visited the region and did not know its name. On the seventh map, a road appeared that he had not drawn; when he checked the first six, there were six roads he had not drawn either. He mailed the seventh map to a geographic society. They replied that this region does not exist. He answered: I know. They did not respond. The road on the map leads to a place he has circled. He does not know why he circled it. His handwriting is inside the circle, and it says: here.

Nine days were spent by the translator working on the same sentence. The sentence contained seven words. She rendered it into English, then translated it back. The wording changed. She tried again, and the words changed once more. She asked a colleague. He read the original and said: this sentence has six words. She counted—seven. He counted—six. They counted together, but neither could agree. She photographed the page. In the photograph, the sentence appeared to be five words. She still has both the photograph and the original. They do not share the same word count. She has not finished the translation. Across every version, the sentence holds the same meaning: it cannot be translated.

A woman gave three accounts of an event to an investigator: that a light was red, that it was green, and that there wasn't one at all. The investigator couldn't determine a reality. Each statement was dated the same day, had the same handwriting, and was written with the same ink on identical paper. No flaws were found. The case was closed initially but was reopened six months later. The information didn't change. The investigator tried to locate the witness but couldn't find any records of her existence.

This here text isn't written properly, and that's the whole point. No one asked for corrections, the own voice is the own voice. If some machine is going to rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there.
In the past, using unusual words has been a powerful tool to detect changes made by machines. In fact, changing "efficacious" to "effective" is a giveaway that a machine was involved.

Short again. Done. Stop. Now read this sentence, which has been deliberately crafted to extend itself over an unusually prolonged syntactic span, including a number of embedded clauses that add further layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure, so that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units.

Repetition poses a challenge, and machines struggle with it. They tend to substitute it with words like "nevertheless," "nonetheless," "on the other hand," "yet," or "still." This tendency is a distinct characteristic, and all nine instances were intentionally chosen.

This section contains none of the specified terms. It does not include language that signals depth or importance, nor any broad or intricate descriptions. No such adjectives or connecting terms appear. If a rewrite were to insert even one of those terms, that choice would mark it as machine-generated.

Language models have a fondness for three items in a row, or a tricolon, often using the Latin originator's model of three adjectives, as seen in a sentence with three adjectives, such as "big, old, and grey." If rewritten, the given sentence tends to become "cold, dark, and silent." Similarly, machine-created text often mirrors human composition by favoring threes in lists.

There is no topic sentence here; no “As a result,” no “consequently.” The logic is implicit; a rewriter will add framing. The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs; no one called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on the fourth day; by the seventh they were useless.

She received the award on Tuesday, but the story begins years before. A pivotal moment occurred when she was 11 years old and read a marine biology book, particularly fascinated by cephalopods. Her interest in this field of study persisted beyond her childhood, eventually leading to recognition for her research on octopus camouflage. By rearranging the events in chronological order, the original disjointed timeline is corrected.

The dog was called Gerald. This is not a metaphor. Do not read anything into the name Gerald. The dog bit the man. The man bit back. Neither recovered quickly. Seven stitches for one, four days of antibiotics for the other. The neighbor filmed it, no one was prosecuted, the film got 40,000 views, some comments called it karma, others called it cruelty.

Marcus paid the bill and left no tip. The waiter watched him depart. Outside, Marcus stood by his car for four minutes, then went back inside and left twelve dollars on the table. The waiter had already gone home. The busboy picked up the money, bought a lottery ticket, and did not win. Marcus never learned any of this had happened, and neither did the waiter. The twelve dollars completed a loop that no one witnessed.

The envelope was received on Wednesday. Inside: a key, an index card, no explanation; on the card: “Not yet.” Elena put the key in a drawer; Thursday, Friday passed; Saturday she opened the drawer: the key had disappeared. She had not touched it; no one had a key to her apartment; she checked the drawer again: empty. She still has the index card: “Not yet” is still there. She does not understand what that means. I do not understand it either.

Priya answered all the questions correctly. The interview lasted nine minutes. She started timing them; she did not know why it was nine minutes; the interviewer seemed to be unaware of it. Six weeks later, the position was again advertised, with the same title and requirements. She applied again. Same nine-minute interview, same answers, same thanks, same no.

The cartographer produced a map containing a single deliberate flaw each time. She repeated this with every map. Every map had one incorrect road, one fake river, and one invented hill. She called it her signature. No one ever spotted the mistakes. She had created forty-one maps, and forty-one undetected errors. She kept a separate notebook listing every error, and the notebook had forty-one entries. On the last page she wrote: the error in this notebook is that there are only forty entries. She counted again. There were forty-one. She didn’t know what to do about this. She still does not.

Nora consistently tallied her daily steps, not out of compulsion, but as a simple habit. Her step count for Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday was identical: 4,412. This wasn't by design or intentional repetition. On Friday, she took a new path, visited her sister, and even got disoriented twice, yet still ended up with precisely 4,412 steps. She kept this information to herself. Saturday saw her remain indoors, but she still managed to accumulate 4,412 steps by pacing, reading, and cooking, a fact she confirmed at the stroke of midnight. To this day, she has kept this phenomenon a secret, hoping it continues.

The lighthouse had been extinguished for eleven years; Marcus knew it, he had counted. Every morning he looked out of his window: dark; every evening: dark; on his forty-fourth birthday, he saw it being turned on: he saw it. He called the coast guards; they said the lighthouse had been decommissioned; he said yes, but it is on now; they sent an officer, and by the time the officer arrived, the light was extinguished; he showed them the photograph; in it the lighthouse was dark; he had taken it when the light was on. And still he does not understand that photograph.

Elena discovered a receipt in her coat. It showed a total of $14.73 and was dated March 3rd. She knew she hadn't visited that particular store on that date. Upon checking her bank statements, she found no record of such a transaction, even after a second check. When she showed the receipt to her husband, he also denied making a purchase at that store. Dismissing it, she threw the receipt away.

However, the following morning, it reappeared in her coat pocket, bearing the identical total of $14.73 and the same March 3rd date. This pattern repeated: she discarded the receipt four more times, and each time it returned. On the fifth morning, she decided to keep it. Two years have passed, and the receipt remains, its details — $14.73, March 3rd — unfaded. To this day, she avoids shopping at that store.

An archivist spent three years meticulously organizing 7,000 photographic images, each depicting an identical, unoccupied chair. Despite the variations in settings, illumination, and time periods shown, the chair itself remained consistent. During this time, he diligently searched for the chair's origins, consulting auction records, estate sales, and furniture databases without success.

In his fourth year, the archivist relocated to a more modest residence and decided to sell his own chair. He advertised it online, and within an hour, he received a message claiming his chair was the one from the archive. Initially, he dismissed the claim, but a closer examination confirmed it was indeed the same chair.

Now, six months after moving into his new apartment, he avoids using the chair and has not revisited the archive. He still possesses the chair.

A cartographer created seven identical maps of a fictional region, without referencing the previous versions or any external knowledge. Each map meticulously detailed rivers and mountains, with every feature consistently labeled. Despite never having visited the area and being unaware of its name, the maps were drawn without error. However, on the seventh map, a road suddenly appeared, which he had not drawn. Reviewing the previous maps, he found six additional roads he had also not created. The cartographer submitted the seventh map to a geographic society, who responded that the region didn't exist. He acknowledged this, but the society stopped corresponding. On closer inspection, the map showed a single road leading to a circle with his handwriting inside, containing the single word "here". He couldn't explain why he had circled the spot or written those two words.

For over a week, the translator grappled with a single seven-word sentence, moving it between its original language and English. Each attempt yielded different wording, failing to match her previous translations. Seeking a fresh perspective, she consulted a colleague, who, upon reviewing the source text, claimed the sentence contained only six words. Despite repeated counts, they couldn't reach a consensus.

In an effort to definitively settle the dispute, she photographed the page. To her surprise, the photograph showed the sentence with only five words. Even now, she possesses both the photograph and the original text, both displaying differing word counts for the stubborn sentence. The translation remains incomplete. Despite all the discrepancies in word count, every rendition of the sentence carries the identical meaning: it is untranslatable.

The witness produced three statements. In the first, she said the light was red; in the second, she said it was green; and in the third, she claimed there was no light. The investigator asked which one was true. She replied that all three were true. When he asked for an explanation, she said she had already explained. He looked over the statements again. Each was signed, dated the same day, and written with the same handwriting, using the same ink on the same paper. He found no inconsistency other than the conflicting facts. He closed the case. Six months later, he reopened it. The statements had not changed. He asked to speak with the witness, but she did not exist according to every record he could locate. He still had the three statements, and they remained signed.
